title: Google Maps Routing — System Design
description: Design the routing and map-serving backend: compute the fastest route between two points over a continental road network in milliseconds, serve map tiles, and incorporate live traffic. The themes are road-graph modeling, making shortest-path fast at scale via preprocessing, tile serving, and a live-traffic pipeline.

nodes.label: Maps Client
nodes.description: Requests routes/ETAs and renders map tiles.

nodes.label: API Gateway
nodes.description: Routes route, tile, and traffic requests.

nodes.label: Routing Service
nodes.description: Computes shortest/fastest path between two points.

nodes.label: Road Graph Store
nodes.description: The road network: nodes (intersections) and weighted edges.

nodes.label: Contraction Hierarchy / Preprocessed Index
nodes.description: Precomputed shortcuts enabling fast long-distance queries.

nodes.label: Tile Service
nodes.description: Serves pre-rendered map tiles by zoom/coordinate.

nodes.label: Traffic Service
nodes.description: Maintains live edge speeds from probe data.

nodes.label: GPS Probe Stream
nodes.description: Anonymized live location probes from devices.

nodes.label: Traffic Aggregator
nodes.description: Aggregates probes into per-edge live speeds.

functional: Compute the fastest route between two locations (addresses or coordinates).
Return route geometry, distance, and ETA; support turn-by-turn.
Serve map tiles for rendering at various zoom levels.
Incorporate live traffic into ETAs and route choice.
nonFunctional: Low routing latency (tens of ms) even for cross-country routes.
Scale to a continental graph (hundreds of millions of nodes/edges) and high query volume.
Tiles served fast globally (mostly static, CDN-cached).
Traffic reasonably fresh (minutes) and reflected in routing.
Mostly read-heavy; the graph changes slowly, traffic changes continuously.
Privacy: GPS probes are opt-in, anonymized, short-retention; operators see only aggregated edge speeds, never raw traces.

capacity.label: Graph size
capacity.note: ~50–80 B/edge -> ~50 GB base graph; CH shortcuts add ~1.5–2x edges. Fits in RAM per region shard, not one machine continent-wide.

capacity.label: Route QPS
capacity.note: p99 < 100 ms budget split: gateway ~5 ms, geocode/snap ~10 ms, CH query ~20 ms, traffic overlay ~15 ms, shortcut unpack + geometry ~30 ms.

capacity.label: Tile reads
capacity.note: Mostly static; only ~10–50k/sec origin QPS after cache misses (cold viewports, new tile versions).

capacity.label: Probe ingestion
capacity.note: Aggregated in 1–5 min windows into per-edge speeds; ~1–5M edge-speed updates/min. Sparse roads fall back to historical speed.

capacity.label: Freshness SLOs
capacity.value: traffic 2–5 min; closures sec–min; base graph hrs–days
capacity.note: Three distinct cadences: live speeds, urgent overlays (closures/incidents), and offline base-graph + CH rebuilds.

capacity.label: Storage / replication
capacity.note: Per-region shard ~5–20 GB held in RAM, 3x replicated read-only across the fleet for the immutable versioned index.

api.description: Compute the fastest route between two points, traffic-aware, with turn-by-turn.
api.response: { "route_id": "r_8f2", "distanceKm": 615, "duration": 21000, "duration_in_traffic": 23400, "polyline": "_p~iF~ps|U...", "legs": [ { "steps": [ { "maneuver": "turn-right", "instruction": "Turn right onto US-101 S", "distanceM": 1200, "durationS": 75, "polyline": "..." } ] } ], "warnings": ["toll road on alternative"], "graph_version": "g-2026-06-01", "traffic_version": "t-1733650200" }

api.description: Fetch a versioned map tile by style/version/zoom/coordinate (CDN-served, immutable).
api.request: style=streets, v=2026-06-01 (tile version), z/x/y, fmt=pbf (vector) or png (raster)
api.response: Tile bytes (vector pbf or raster png). Headers: Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable; Content-Type per fmt. New map data = new version path, so URLs are immutable and CDN never serves stale tiles.

api.description: Resolve an address/place to coordinates + nearest graph node (snapping).

dataModel.name: road_graph (edge)
dataModel.note: Directed weighted edges (road segments) keyed by a stable edge_id so traffic, geometry, and turn-by-turn all attach to it.

fields.name: geometry_ref
fields.type: polyline ref (for drawing the route line)

fields.name: access_flags
fields.type: bitmask (toll, highway, ferry, hov, vehicle class)

dataModel.name: ch_index
dataModel.note: Preprocessed contraction-hierarchy shortcuts; each shortcut records the edges it skips so the route can be unpacked back to base geometry and maneuvers.

fields.name: index_version, graph_version
fields.type: string (CH artifact id + compatible base graph id)

dataModel.name: edge_speed (traffic)
dataModel.note: Live + time-bucketed speed per edge with confidence, so stale/sparse edges fall back to historical speed rather than free-flow.

patterns.name: Graph model of roads
patterns.what: Model the network as weighted directed edges between intersection nodes.
patterns.group: Model Roads as a Weighted Graph

patterns.name: Preprocessing for fast shortest-path
patterns.what: Precompute shortcuts (contraction hierarchies / hub labels) so queries skip most of the graph.
patterns.whenToUse: Repeated shortest-path queries on a slowly-changing large graph.

patterns.name: Pre-rendered tiles + CDN
patterns.what: Pre-render map tiles by zoom/coordinate and serve from CDN.
patterns.whenToUse: Map display where most content is static and read-heavy.

patterns.name: Probe aggregation (live traffic)
patterns.what: Aggregate anonymized GPS probes into per-edge live speeds.
patterns.whenToUse: Deriving real-time conditions from crowd data.

patterns.name: Geo partitioning / region sharding
patterns.what: Partition the graph and tiles by geographic region for locality and scale.

patterns.name: Dynamic edge weights
patterns.what: Combine static base travel time with a live-traffic multiplier at query time.

steps.title: 1. Naive: Dijkstra/A* Over the Whole Graph Per Request
description: Start with the textbook answer: load the road network into a graph and run Dijkstra (or A* with a straight-line heuristic) from the origin to the destination on every request, expanding edges by travel time until the destination is settled. For a single city this is correct and even fast enough.

It fails on latency at continental scale. A long route — say coast to coast — forces the search to settle a huge share of hundreds of millions of nodes before it reaches the destination: plain Dijkstra can settle tens of millions of nodes and take seconds, whereas a CH query touches only a few thousand nodes and answers in single-digit milliseconds. A* helps near the goal but still explores far too much of the graph on long routes, and it must do this work from scratch for every query.

The saving grace is that the road graph changes only slowly (roads are added over days, not seconds). That makes it worth paying a large one-time, offline cost to precompute structures that let online queries skip almost all of the graph. So the baseline motivates the next two steps: first pin down the graph model, then make shortest-path fast with preprocessing.

view.caption: The maps client calls the API gateway, which routes a route(A,B) request to the routing service that reads edge weights from the road graph store.

steps.decisionPrompt: Plain Dijkstra over the full graph answers any route correctly. Why does it blow the latency budget for a cross-country trip but not for a single city?
whyNow: Running Dijkstra per request is the honest baseline; seeing it settle too much of a continental graph is exactly what justifies the graph model and the heavy offline preprocessing in the next two steps.

recap.after: Correct routes via Dijkstra/A* over the full graph per request.
recap.newRisk: Settling most of a 100M-node graph per long route is far too slow — must model the graph cleanly, then preprocess shortcuts.

traps.trap: Answering 'run Dijkstra/A* per request' and stopping there.
traps.why: On a continental graph a long route settles a huge fraction of hundreds of millions of nodes — seconds per query, not milliseconds.
traps.instead: Keep Dijkstra as the baseline, then exploit the slow-changing graph with offline preprocessing (contraction hierarchies).

steps.title: 2. Model Roads as a Weighted Graph
description: The foundation: model the road network as a directed weighted graph. Nodes are intersections; edges are road segments with a weight (free-flow travel time, derived from length + speed limit + road class). One-way streets and turn restrictions are encoded in edge directionality/penalties.
A route is a shortest-path query over this graph by travel-time weight. The graph store holds nodes, edges, and geometry (for drawing the route line).
This graph is huge (hundreds of millions of nodes for a continent) but changes slowly — roads rarely change — which is what makes heavy preprocessing viable.

view.caption: The routing service first resolves the endpoints through the geocoding service, then computes the path over edge weights in the road graph store.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you represent roads so 'fastest route' becomes a well-defined query?

concepts.definition: Intersections as nodes, road segments as directed weighted edges (travel time).
concepts.whyItMatters: Turns routing into a shortest-path problem with standard algorithms.
concepts.example: Edge weight = segment length / speed limit; one-ways are directed edges.
concepts.group: Model Roads as a Weighted Graph

whyNow: The graph model is the foundation; the size + slow-change nature is exactly what justifies the preprocessing in the next step.

recap.after: Roads as a weighted directed graph; routing = shortest path.
recap.newRisk: Plain Dijkstra over hundreds of millions of nodes per query is far too slow.

steps.title: 3. Make Shortest-Path Fast with Preprocessing
description: Naive Dijkstra/A* explores too much of a continental graph to hit a ms latency budget for long routes. Because the graph changes slowly, precompute structures that let queries skip most of it.

Contraction hierarchies: rank nodes and add 'shortcut' edges that bypass less-important nodes, so a long route is found by touching a tiny fraction of the graph (a bidirectional search up the hierarchy). Hub labeling is an even faster (more storage) alternative. Build these offline; queries use the precomputed index.

One subtlety: the CH query returns a path of shortcuts, not base roads. Each shortcut records the edges it skipped, so after finding the path you must unpack shortcuts back into base edges to produce the actual road geometry and turn-by-turn maneuvers — stopping at 'shortest distance' isn't a usable route.
This preprocessing is the key idea: trade offline build time + storage for ms-level online queries on a massive graph.

view.caption: The routing service queries a contraction-hierarchy index of precomputed shortcuts, built offline from the road graph store, instead of searching the raw graph.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why doesn't plain Dijkstra/A* work for a cross-country route at ms latency, and what fixes it?

concepts.definition: Precomputed node ranking + shortcut edges enabling near-instant long-distance shortest paths.
concepts.whyItMatters: Cuts a continental query from exploring millions of nodes to a few thousand.
concepts.example: Bidirectional search up the hierarchy finds NYC->LA in ms.

whyNow: Preprocessing is the intellectual core; naming contraction hierarchies / hub labels and the offline-build tradeoff is the senior signal.

recap.newRisk: Routes computed, but the map itself must be drawn — tiles.

traps.trap: Running Dijkstra over the full graph per request.
traps.why: Touches far too many nodes to meet the latency budget on long routes.
traps.instead: Precompute (contraction hierarchies / hub labels) and query the index.

steps.title: 3a. Preprocessing Choices: CH vs Hub Labeling vs A*
description: Several techniques make long-distance queries fast; they trade build time and storage against query speed. Contraction hierarchies add shortcut edges by importance so a bidirectional search touches a tiny fraction of the graph — moderate build, moderate storage, fast queries, and the common production choice.

Hub labeling precomputes a label set per node so a query is essentially a label intersection — the fastest queries, but the largest storage. A*-with-landmarks (ALT) needs the least preprocessing (just landmark distances) but explores more of the graph per query.

The decision is a build-time/storage/query-latency triangle. For a continental road graph that changes slowly, contraction hierarchies (or hub labeling where storage allows) win; ALT suits graphs that change too often to preprocess heavily.

view.caption: The routing service answers queries from the contraction-hierarchy shortcuts, which are derived from the underlying road graph store.

options.name: Contraction hierarchies (default)
options.description: Nodes are contracted by importance, adding shortcut edges so a bidirectional query touches only a tiny high-rank slice of the continental graph.
pros: Adds shortcut edges by node importance so a bidirectional search touches a tiny fraction of the continental graph.
Moderate build time and storage — the common production sweet spot for road networks.
Shortcuts compose cleanly, so cross-region long routes reuse the few high-rank spanning shortcuts.
cons: Queries are slower than hub labeling's near-constant lookups.
Live traffic changes edge weights, so CH needs a customizable variant to avoid full re-preprocessing.
Rebuilding the hierarchy when the map changes takes hours.

view.caption: Contraction hierarchies: the routing service queries the precomputed shortcut index built from the road graph store for near-instant paths.

options.name: Hub labeling (label-set lookup)
options.description: Each node precomputes a set of hub labels with distances, so a shortest-path query reduces to intersecting the two endpoints' label sets.
pros: Fastest queries: a shortest-path query reduces to intersecting two precomputed label sets.
Query latency is essentially independent of route length — great for cross-country trips.
Simple query logic once labels are built.
cons: Largest storage of the three — label sets per node are big for a continental graph.
Build is heavy, and labels must be rebuilt when the map changes.
Storage cost can dominate, especially when partitioned across regions.

view.caption: Hub labeling: the routing service intersects per-node label sets in a hub-label index built from the graph, answering a query with a label lookup.

options.name: A*-with-landmarks (ALT)
options.description: Only a handful of landmark distance tables are precomputed; queries run A* using those distances as an admissible heuristic to guide the search.
pros: Least preprocessing: only a handful of landmark distance tables to precompute.
Adapts naturally to changed edge weights, so live traffic is easier to fold in.
Smallest storage footprint of the three techniques.
cons: Slowest queries — it still expands a large portion of the graph for long routes.
Continental coast-to-coast routes can miss the ms latency budget.
Landmark choice strongly affects quality and needs tuning.

view.caption: A*-with-landmarks: the routing service expands graph edges directly while consulting landmark tables for lower-bound estimates to guide the search.

steps.decisionPrompt: CH vs hub labeling vs A*-landmarks: how do build time, storage, and query latency trade off?

concepts.definition: CH = balanced; hub labeling = fastest queries, most storage; ALT = least preprocessing, slower queries.
concepts.whyItMatters: Picks the right speed/storage/freshness point for the graph's size and update rate.
concepts.example: OSRM/Google use contraction hierarchies; research systems use hub labeling for sub-microsecond queries.
concepts.group: Preprocessing Choices: CH vs Hub Labeling vs A*

traps.trap: Assuming preprocessing is free or one-size-fits-all.
traps.why: Hub labeling can need enormous storage; heavy preprocessing is wasted if the graph changes constantly.
traps.instead: Match the technique to graph size, update frequency, and storage budget.

recap.after: A specific technique chosen against the build/storage/latency triangle.
recap.newRisk: Heavier preprocessing is harder to refresh when the map changes (handled by offline rebuilds).

steps.title: 4. Serving Map Tiles
description: Rendering the map is a separate, mostly-static problem. The world is divided into tiles by zoom level and x/y coordinate; tiles are pre-rendered (raster or vector) from map data and stored.

Serve tiles from a CDN — they're static, read-heavy, and identical for all users, so the CDN absorbs essentially all the load and the origin is rarely touched. The client requests tiles by style/version/z/x/y as the user pans/zooms, with long-lived immutable cache headers.

Tiles are re-rendered only when the underlying map data changes (rare). Publishing a new tile_version makes a fresh immutable URL path rather than purging the CDN, so there's never a stale-cache window; the origin sits behind the CDN with request coalescing so a popular cold area doesn't stampede it. This is the metadata/blob/CDN pattern applied to map imagery.

view.caption: Tile requests go to the tile service, but clients mostly fetch pre-rendered tiles straight from the CDN, which falls back to the tile store on a miss.

options.name: Pre-rendered tiles on a CDN (default)
options.description: The world is rendered offline into a z/x/y tile pyramid stored once; clients fetch static tiles by coordinate from a CDN that serves nearly all the traffic.
pros: Tiles are identical for all users and rarely change, so a CDN absorbs essentially all the load.
Serving is a static GET by z/x/y — the origin is touched only on a cold cache.
Cheap and fast; rendering cost is paid offline, not per request.
cons: Re-rendering the whole tile pyramid after a map change is expensive.
Raster tiles bake in style/zoom, so restyling means a full re-render.
Large storage for every zoom level of the world.

view.caption: Pre-rendered tiles are served from the CDN, with the tile service and origin tile store backing it only on cache misses.

options.name: Vector tiles rendered on the client
options.description: Compact vector tile data is shipped from a CDN and the client rasterizes, styles, and zooms it on-device rather than fetching pre-rendered images.
pros: Ship compact vector data once; the client restyles, rotates, and zooms smoothly without re-fetching.
Far fewer tiles to store since one vector tile serves many styles and intermediate zooms.
Crisp at any DPI and supports dynamic theming (dark mode, traffic overlay).
cons: Pushes rendering work onto the device — heavier client CPU/GPU and bigger app.
Older or low-end clients render slowly or inconsistently.
More complex client toolchain than serving ready-made raster images.

view.caption: The client fetches vector tiles from the CDN and renders them locally, with the CDN pulling from the vector tile store on a miss.

options.name: Render tiles on demand at the origin
options.description: The tile service rasterizes each requested z/x/y tile at the origin per request from live map data, with no pre-rendered pyramid.
pros: No tile pyramid to pre-render or store — generate exactly what is requested.
Map edits appear immediately, with no re-render-and-publish step.
Supports per-request customization (highlight a route, custom styling).
cons: Rendering per request is CPU-heavy and blows the read-heavy, static-content latency budget.
The render origin becomes a bottleneck under global pan/zoom traffic.
Defeats the CDN, which thrives on identical static tiles.

view.caption: On-demand rendering: the client asks the tile service to render z/x/y now, fetching source data from the tile store per request.

steps.decisionPrompt: Map tiles are the same for everyone and rarely change. What serves them?

concepts.definition: Pre-rendered z/x/y tiles served from CDN edge.
concepts.whyItMatters: Handles massive read fanout for map display with near-zero origin load.
concepts.example: Pan the map -> fetch tiles at z=14 around the viewport from CDN.

whyNow: Tile serving is a distinct, large part of a maps system; recognizing it as static CDN content is the clean separation from routing.

recap.after: Map imagery served fast and globally via CDN tiles.
recap.newRisk: Static base weights ignore reality — ETAs and routes must reflect live traffic.

flows.name: Tile fetch: CDN hit vs origin fill
flows.note: Tiles are identical for everyone and rarely change, so the CDN serves almost all reads and only misses fall through to the tile store.

steps.title: 5. Live Traffic from GPS Probes
description: Free-flow travel times are wrong during congestion. Devices send opt-in, anonymized GPS probes. The aggregator does map matching — snapping each noisy probe to the most likely road edge, filtering bad GPS fixes, and deduping by anonymized session — then computes a live speed per edge with a confidence/sample_count, updated every 1–5 minutes.

Routing uses dynamic edge weights: at query time the router overlays the live multiplier on the static base time for the candidate corridor, so ETAs and the chosen route reflect current conditions. When an edge has too few probes or stale data, it falls back to the historical speed for that time-of-day and road class — not free-flow — and incidents/closures push an override (banned/heavily penalized) within seconds.

Crucially, live traffic doesn't require re-preprocessing the whole contraction hierarchy. Plain CH assumes fixed weights, so the practical approaches are: customizable CH (a cheap per-update customization phase keeps the hierarchy valid under new weights), time-dependent CH for departure-time ETAs, or a hybrid that runs CH on base weights to find a candidate corridor then repairs it with local A* under live weights. All keep the expensive offline build untouched.
Privacy is first-class: probes are short-retention, never exposed as raw traces; operators and the routing service read only aggregated per-edge speeds.

view.caption: Clients stream GPS probes into the probe stream; the traffic aggregator turns them into per-edge speeds in the traffic service, which the routing service reads as live weights.

options.name: Dynamic edge weights at query time (default)
options.description: GPS probes are aggregated into per-edge live speeds; the router combines the static base time with a live multiplier at query time without rebuilding the index.
pros: Combine the static base time with a live-traffic multiplier at query time, so ETAs reflect current jams.
Live speeds update every few minutes without re-running the expensive preprocessing.
Probe ingestion stays decoupled from the read-only routing index.
cons: Plain contraction hierarchies assume fixed weights, so you need a customizable CH (or A*) to honor live weights.
Applying multipliers per query adds work to the latency budget.
A bad probe-to-edge match can distort a segment's live speed.

view.caption: Dynamic weights at query time: aggregated per-edge speeds feed the traffic service, and the routing service overlays those live speeds onto its search.

options.name: Re-preprocess the graph with live weights
options.description: Live traffic speeds are baked into the contraction hierarchy by rerunning preprocessing, so shortcut weights already reflect current conditions at query time.
pros: Queries stay maximally fast because traffic is already baked into the shortcut weights.
No per-query multiplier logic on the hot path.
Routes are guaranteed optimal under the weights used to build the index.
cons: Contraction hierarchy builds take hours, so you can't rebuild every few minutes for live traffic.
Forces a choice between cheap freshness and full re-preprocessing — the wrong tradeoff for a 5-minute traffic jam.
Huge compute cost to rebuild a continental index repeatedly.

view.caption: Re-preprocessing: the aggregator feeds live weights into a re-preprocess job that rebuilds the contraction-hierarchy index the routing service queries.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you make routes and ETAs reflect a traffic jam that started 5 minutes ago?

concepts.definition: Aggregate anonymized GPS probes into per-edge live speeds applied as dynamic weights.
concepts.whyItMatters: Makes ETAs and routing reflect real conditions from crowd data.
concepts.example: Edge live speed 40% of free-flow -> ETA and route avoid it.

traps.trap: Treating a probe as a direct edge-speed measurement.
traps.why: Raw GPS is noisy and ambiguous between parallel roads; without map matching and quality filtering you assign speeds to the wrong segment.
traps.instead: Map-match probes to candidate edges, filter bad fixes, and weight by confidence/sample_count.

traps.trap: Falling back to free-flow speed when traffic data is missing or stale.
traps.why: On a busy road with sparse probes, free-flow gives wildly optimistic ETAs.
traps.instead: Fall back to historical speed by time-of-day and road class; only use free-flow as a last resort.

traps.trap: Storing or exposing raw GPS traces.
traps.why: Individual location histories are highly sensitive; retaining them is a privacy and compliance liability.
traps.instead: Keep probes opt-in, anonymized, short-retention; expose only aggregated per-edge speeds.

whyNow: Live traffic is what makes maps useful and is the dynamic counterpart to the static preprocessed graph; applying it without re-preprocessing is the key nuance.

recap.after: Traffic-aware routing from live probe data.
recap.newRisk: The graph, tiles, and traffic are continental-scale — they must be partitioned and scaled.

flows.name: Probe ingestion -> live edge speeds -> routing
flows.note: Anonymized GPS probes are aggregated into per-edge live speeds the router applies as dynamic weights at query time.

steps.title: 6. Scaling: Region Partitioning
description: A continental graph and tile set are too big for one machine. Partition by geography: shard the road graph and tiles into regions, serve them from region-local clusters/CDN edges, and route within/across regions.

The hard part is boundaries — routes regularly cross them. Each region owns its local graph; border vertices on the cut are duplicated/coordinated between neighbors, and a thin inter-region overlay graph holds the high-rank CH shortcuts that span regions. A cross-region query plans on the overlay first (source region -> border -> destination region) and only unpacks locally inside the two endpoint regions, so it never fans out synchronously to every region.

Partition by traffic/topology, not raw administrative borders, and split hot metros (a dense city can dominate load and shouldn't be one shard with a quiet rural region). Probe ingestion and traffic aggregation are also regional.

Geo partitioning gives locality (low latency near the user), independent scaling, and fault isolation. Degradation is graceful: if one region's graph shard, traffic feed, or tile origin is unavailable, routes wholly inside other regions are unaffected, traffic there falls back to historical/free-flow, and only routes that must traverse the failed region are degraded or rejected.

view.caption: At scale the routing service is backed by a partitioned road graph and contraction index, while tiles are served from the tile store via the CDN.

options.name: Geographic region partitioning (default)
options.description: The road graph and tiles are sharded by geography and served from region-local clusters and CDN edges; cross-region routes stitch together via the high-rank CH shortcuts.
pros: Most routes are local and served entirely by a region-local cluster and CDN edge.
Each region's graph and tiles fit on its own cluster, so no single machine holds everything.
Long inter-region routes plan on a thin overlay graph of high-rank cross-region shortcuts, then unpack locally — no synchronous fanout to every region.
Failure is isolated: a dead region only degrades routes that traverse it.
cons: Border vertices must be duplicated/coordinated and an inter-region overlay graph maintained.
Region boundaries should follow traffic/topology, not administrative lines, and hot metros need their own splits.
Probe ingestion and traffic aggregation must also be partitioned per region.

view.caption: Geographic partitioning splits the road graph and contraction index by region, so each routing-service shard owns a slice of the graph.

options.name: Replicate the whole graph to every node
options.description: Every routing node holds a full copy of the continental graph and index, so any node can answer any query with no cross-partition stitching.
pros: Any node answers any route, so there is no cross-partition stitching at all.
Routing logic stays simple — one immutable graph everywhere.
Trivial load balancing across identical read-only replicas.
cons: Every node must hold the entire continental graph and index — capped by one machine's memory.
Doesn't actually solve 'the graph doesn't fit on one machine', the core driver here.
Rebuilding and shipping the full index to every node is expensive.

view.caption: Full replication keeps the entire road graph and contraction index on every routing-service node, so any node answers any query without cross-shard hops.

steps.decisionPrompt: The graph doesn't fit on one machine. How do you partition it, and what about routes that cross partitions?
whyNow: Geo partitioning is how a planet-scale graph/tile set is served with locality; handling cross-region routes via the hierarchy is the scaling nuance.

concepts.definition: Partition graph/tiles/traffic by geography with duplicated border nodes; long routes plan on a thin inter-region overlay of high-rank shortcuts, then unpack locally.
concepts.whyItMatters: Locality + independent scaling + fault isolation at planet scale, without synchronous fanout across regions.
concepts.example: West-coast routes served regionally; NYC->LA plans on the overlay then unpacks in the NY and CA shards.

recap.newRisk: Graph rebuilds and preprocessing must be managed as the map evolves; serving must stay available.

steps.title: 7. Graph Updates, Caching, and Reliability
description: Treat the graph and CH index as immutable, versioned artifacts. The build pipeline ingests map changes, validates topology, builds the CH (which carries the compatible graph_version), runs a route-regression suite, then publishes. Roll it out by canarying a region and watching route metrics (latency, failure rate, distance/ETA deltas); roll back to the previous version on regression. The routing service is read-only; a request pins one graph_version + index_version through the CH query, shortcut unpacking, and geometry expansion so it never mixes incompatible artifacts.

Separate cadences: urgent changes (closures, restrictions, incidents) push through a fast overlay/restriction store in seconds–minutes as edge overrides, while the expensive base-graph + CH rebuild stays on a slow (hours–days) offline cadence. Tiles are versioned immutable assets — a new tile_version path, not a CDN purge.

Cache aggressively: route cache keys include origin/destination cells, mode, avoid flags, graph_version, and a traffic-freshness bucket; tiles and geocoding results cache too. The routing service is stateless and horizontally scaled; the preprocessed index is replicated read-only across the fleet.

Reliability: the graph/index and tiles are derived, rebuildable artifacts; traffic degrades gracefully (fall back to historical, then free-flow weights, if the probe pipeline lags) so routing still works, just less traffic-aware.

view.caption: Operationally the routing service depends on the contraction index, graph store, and traffic service, with the index periodically rebuilt from the graph.

steps.decisionPrompt: Preprocessing takes hours and the map just changed. How do you update without downtime?
whyNow: Closing on offline rebuild + caching + graceful traffic fallback shows the system stays fresh, fast, and available — the operational reality.

concepts.definition: Graph, CH index, and tiles are published as immutable versioned artifacts; a request pins one compatible (graph_version, index_version) through query, unpack, and geometry expansion.
concepts.whyItMatters: Version pinning is a correctness property, not just ops — mismatched graph/index or a half-published rollout yields invalid routes.
concepts.example: g-2026-06-01 + matching CH; canary one region, watch route metrics, roll back on regression.

recap.after: Versioned offline rebuilds, heavy caching, graceful traffic degradation.
recap.newRisk: Rebuild cadence vs map freshness and probe-pipeline lag — bounded by versioned swaps and free-flow fallback.

bottlenecks.mitigation: Offline build on a cadence; atomic versioned index swap; incremental/customizable CH for traffic.

bottlenecks.issue: Urgent closures can't wait for a base rebuild.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Push closures/restrictions as edge overrides through a fast overlay store in seconds–minutes; base graph/CH rebuild stays slow.
Route cache keyed by od-cells/mode/avoid/graph_version/freshness; CDN for versioned immutable tiles.

failureDrills.scenario: The traffic probe pipeline lags or fails.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Routing falls back to historical then free-flow base weights; routes still compute, just less congestion-aware.
failureDrills.mitigation: Static base graph as the always-available fallback; traffic as an overlay.

failureDrills.scenario: A bad graph/index is published (broken topology, regressed routes) or a region's index publishes only partially.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Canary route-metric regression halts the rollout; the fleet keeps serving the previous pinned version; no request mixes a half-published index.
failureDrills.mitigation: Route-regression suite + canary by region before fleet-wide; immutable versioned artifacts with one-click rollback; requests pin a single graph_version/index_version end to end.

flows.name: Offline build -> validate -> canary publish
flows.note: Map changes are built into immutable versioned artifacts, regression-tested, then canaried by region before the fleet swaps to the new version.

messages.label: canary one region, watch route metrics

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Maps Routing & Serving
finalDesign.description: Roads are a weighted directed graph; an offline-built contraction-hierarchy index lets the stateless routing service answer cross-continental shortest-path queries in milliseconds. A request pins one compatible (graph_version, index_version) through the CH query, then unpacks shortcuts back into base edges to produce route geometry and turn-by-turn maneuvers. Live GPS probes are map-matched and aggregated into per-edge speeds the router overlays as dynamic weights (traffic-aware ETAs without re-preprocessing), falling back to historical then free-flow speed when traffic is sparse or stale; raw probes are short-lived, privacy-controlled, and never read directly by routing. Closures/restrictions take a fast overlay path while base graph/CH artifacts are rebuilt, regression-tested, and canaried offline. Map tiles are versioned immutable assets served from CDN with origin protection. Graph, tiles, and traffic are geo-partitioned for locality and scale, with long routes planned on an inter-region overlay/border graph of high-rank shortcuts before local unpacking; a failed region only degrades routes that traverse it.

view.caption: End to end: the gateway fans route requests to the routing service (geocoding, contraction index, graph, live traffic) and tile requests to the tile service and CDN, with GPS probes feeding the traffic pipeline.

functional.how: CH-accelerated shortest path over the road graph.
Unpack CH shortcuts to base edges, expand geometry, emit legs/steps/maneuvers with traffic-aware duration.

nonFunctional.how: Immutable versioned artifacts, route-regression + canary rollout, fast closure overlays, graceful fallback.
Opt-in, anonymized, short-retention probes; only aggregated per-edge speeds are read by routing/operators.

say: Confirm routing + tiles + live traffic over a continental graph.
Pin ms-level routing and the graph's slow-change/large-size nature.
Note tiles are static/read-heavy; traffic is continuous.
Roads as a weighted graph; routing = shortest path.
Tiles via CDN; traffic from probes.
Why Dijkstra fails and how CH/hub-labeling fixes it.
Geo partitioning, offline rebuilds, graceful fallback.
Tradeoffs: preprocessing build time/storage vs query speed, rebuild cadence, traffic freshness.
Mention multi-modal routing and alternatives as extensions.

expectations: Models roads as a graph and runs Dijkstra/A*.
Uses contraction hierarchies for fast routing.
Reasons about CH/hub-labeling tradeoffs and cross-region routing.
Designs offline rebuilds + graceful degradation precisely.

followUps: Contraction hierarchies vs hub labeling vs A* with landmarks — tradeoffs?
How do you incorporate live traffic without rebuilding the preprocessed index each time?
How would you support alternative routes and avoid (tolls, highways)?
How do you route across geographic partitions efficiently?
How would you add multi-modal routing (transit + walking + driving)?
